documents.title: NSF 53 vs 58 is not enough by itself for PFAS decisions
documents.summary: The standard code matters, but the project should still check the exact claim, model, replacement cadence, and cost before treating a product as a fit.

documents.title: PFAS filter annual cost matters because maintenance changes the real decision
documents.summary: Upfront price is the wrong shortcut. Cartridge cadence, replacement cost, and whether the path is even justified determine whether a product is reasonable.

documents.title: Public water vs private well is the first split, not a small detail
documents.summary: These are different evidence systems. Public water begins with utility records. Private wells begin with owner-driven testing and state guidance.

documents.title: How to read your CCR or utility report without turning it into a false household verdict
documents.summary: A CCR is a routing tool, not a magic answer. The engine should use it to establish source, system, timing, and whether a newer PFAS notice or direct utility page exists.

documents.title: Test first vs filter first is mostly a question about evidence quality
documents.summary: Buying first feels faster, but the project should usually force direct evidence ahead of shopping unless an official utility context already exists.

documents.title: Under-sink vs whole-house should start with purpose, not fear
documents.summary: Whole-house is an escalation path, not a default upgrade. For PFAS, the engine should keep ingestion-focused point-of-use visible until a broader whole-home objective is actually justified.

documents.summary: Utility observations, benchmark context, and certified point-of-use next steps for City of Lancaster Water Department.
